fragments.content: Biosimilars are biological products highly similar to an already FDA-approved reference biologic, with no clinically meaningful differences in safety, purity, and potency. Unlike generic small-molecule drugs, biosimilars are not identical due to the complexity of biological manufacturing (living cells, post-translational modifications). The Biologics Price Competition and Innovation Act (BPCIA) of 2010 created an abbreviated approval pathway (351(k)) requiring analytical studies, animal studies, and clinical studies demonstrating similarity. Interchangeable biosimilars meet additional standards allowing pharmacy-level substitution without prescriber intervention.

Biosimilar development follows a stepwise 'totality of evidence' approach: (1) Extensive analytical characterization comparing structure, function, purity, and stability to reference product; (2) Animal studies assessing toxicity and pharmacokinetics; (3) Human PK/PD studies in healthy volunteers or patients demonstrating equivalent exposure and response; (4) Clinical efficacy/safety study in sensitive population using equivalence margins; (5) Immunogenicity assessment. The goal is to demonstrate similarity, not re-establish efficacy. For interchangeability designation, sponsors must provide additional data showing the biosimilar can be alternated/swapped with reference product without increased risk, typically through switching studies with multiple transitions.

Biosimilar approval criteria require: high similarity to reference product despite minor differences in clinically inactive components, same mechanism(s) of action, route of administration, dosage form, and strength as reference product, no clinically meaningful differences in safety, purity, potency across conditions of use, and facility meeting GMP standards. Interchangeability criteria add: biosimilar must be expected to produce same clinical result as reference in any given patient, and for multi-dose products, risk of alternating/switching is not greater than using reference alone. FDA publishes lists of approved biosimilars and interchangeable products. Biosimilars receive 1-year exclusivity from first interchangeable approval. Reference biologics have 12 years of data exclusivity before biosimilar applications can be submitted. Over 40 biosimilars have been approved in the US as of 2024.
